path: 99 Stuff/ETFA25 AAS Paper/ETFA25-000278 Combining Publicly Available SDKs and Code Generation Tools to Streamline the Implementation of AAS Applications.pdf
text: Combining Publicly Available SDKs and Code Generation Tools to Streamline the Implementation of AAS Applications Tom Gneuß ∗, Marko Ristin † Nico Braunisch ∗, Hans Wernher van de Venn ‡, Martin Wollschlaeger ∗, ∗Institute for Applied Computer Science, TUD Dresden Dresden University of Technology, Dresden, Germany {tom.gneuss, nico.braunisch, martin.wollschlaeger}@tu-dresden.de †Computer Science Department, Nazarbayev University, Astana, Kazakhstan marko.ristin@nu.edu.kz ‡Institute of Mechatronic Systems, Zurich University of Applied Sciences, Winterthur, Switzerland vhns@zhaw.ch Abstract—The developer community has made signifi- cant efforts to enable fast and resilient implementations of industrial applications centred around the Asset Ad- ministration Shell (AAS). Software Developer Kits (SDKs) help industry and academia to streamline the development process and reduce errors when working with the AAS metamodel or serving the AAS API. In this work, we analyze established SDKs and other supporting tools that facilitate the development of AAS SDKs and applications. We introduce four workflows with varying degrees of au- tomation that leverage these tools, discussing both current and anticipated challenges related to code generation, along with effective mitigation strategies. These workflows are qualitatively compared regarding their implementation and management effort, required skills, correctness, as well as future-proof maintainability. Based on six study cases of real-world AAS implementations, we ascertain the extent to which these workflows are already being applied in practice. Index Terms—Industry 4.0, Asset Administration Shell, Code Generation, SDK I. INTRODUCTION The vision of Industry 4.0 (I4.0) is an interconnected landscape of machines, devices, and companies heavily relying on interoperability and standardization. The digi- tal twins – a digital representation of an asset throughout its lifecycle – are key for seamless data exchange across stakeholders and support advanced applications such as predictive maintenance, data-driven decisions, and automated bartering [1]. The Asset Administration Shell (AAS) [2] has emerged as the de facto standard for digital twins in I4.0, serving as the single source of truth for asset-related information. The AAS is backed by a metamodel mandating the structure of the asset information [2] as well as an inter- face for accessing and modifying it [3]. Developers rely on Software Development Kits (SDKs), which encap- The work presented here was carried out in the research project ”Fluid 4.0”, publicly funded by the German Federal Ministry for Economic Affairs and Climate Action under the grant 13IK039L Fig. 1. Essential steps in implementing AAS applications following the commonly adopted workflow (see Section II-B, Section II-C). Red arrows indicate steps requiring manual intervention, and can thus be error-prone and should be avoided, if possible. Green arrows highlight potential for improvements of the workflow. Dashed arrows represent future work. sulate commonly used functions, to implement practical industrial AAS-based applications. Typical SDK features include data manipulation, verification, and API-based exchange. The SDKs are sometimes implemented manually, but more often generated (semi-)automatically (see Fig. 1) due to the volume of the specification (e.g., 55 classes and 113 constraints) [4]. Automated SDK generation, aided by code generators, ensures reliable, correct, and complete SDKs while quickly and effortlessly incorpo- rating updates of the AAS metamodel or API definition. The diversity of SDKs poses a major challenge for de- velopers: no single SDK satisfies all needs, due to signif- icant differences in correctness and language support [5], as well as feature sets (see Section II-B). In particular, support for API functionality – such as server stubs and clients – varies widely. Automating the inclusion of API features in SDKs, e.g., using code generators, is integral to ensure correct and quickly adaptable SDKs. This streamlines the creation of applications that serve or consume AAS APIs over HTTP [6], gRPC [7], MQTT [8], and others. In this work, after reviewing relevant existing litera- ture (Section II), we perform a conceptual exploration of how to improve the application development work- flows, including the production of related AAS SDKs extensions both manually or by leveraging model-driven code generation (Section III). We qualitatively assess the advantages and drawbacks of the individual approaches (Section IV), and point out where we anticipate pitfalls – particularly in otherwise efficient generation-based workflows. We propose and validate mitigation strate- gies, demonstrating their feasibility through real-world implementations in the study cases (Section V). Lastly, we discuss our findings and outline their potential future improvements and utilization (Section VII). II. STATE OF THE ART A. The AAS Metamodel and API The supporting foundation of the AAS is its generic metamodel, which allows users to capture a large variety of information with its versatile structures [2]. It defines data types, their composition, and relations, together with several constraints on data elements (e.g., the maximum length of name tags). The AAS of a particular asset is a digital model of the asset adhering to the rules of the metamodel. It is subdivided into Submodels that focus on an aspect of the asset, e.g., a digital nameplate, operational data, etc. Submodels consist of Submodel Elements – atomic nuggets of information, such as Properties, Ranges, References etc., carrying the actual data. Traditionally, exchanging the information modelled in an AAS and its Submodels was done by serializing it as either JSON, XML, or RDF file. The modern, more dynamic approach, known as Type-2-AAS, considers the AAS to be an ever-changing information container that can be continuously updated [17]. It requires a service that represents the AAS and provides access to its data, allowing users to read and manipulate it. These create- read-update-delete (CRUD) operations are defined in the AAS API specification [3]. In the specification, all service definitions are written down in a technology-agnostic manner, i.e., without particular protocols in mind. There is, however, an official mapping of the protocol-agnostic services and operations to respective endpoints and their payload in the HTTP protocol [18]. Other mappings, e.g., to MQTT [8], gRPC [7], and OPC UA [19] are possible but not standardized yet. B. SDKs for Working with the AAS Implementing AAS-based business logic involves interacting with Submodels -— manipulating values, adding elements, or serializing the AAS for exchange. This requires a programmatic representation of meta- model types in the SDK, including their structural con- straints to ensure compliance. Beyond core data handling, the AAS specification defines APIs for repositories, registries, and other AAS- related services. SDKs are expected to ease both server- and client-side implementation by abstracting low-level tasks such as request handling, channel management, serialization, and transport configuration [20]. Summarized in Table I are publicly available SDKs and their main characteristics to provide an overview of their particular strengths and limitations. We refer to previous work, which ascertained the correctness of the individual SDKs: [5] for the metamodel-specific part, and [21] for the API implementations. C. Other Tools for Implementing the AAS Developers are not strictly dependent on SDKs with built-in AAS API functionality to implement servers or clients. As the aas-python-http-client [15] demon- strates, code generators can be used to produce server stubs or client helpers based on formal API definitions. This enables a fast, reliable, repeatable, and automat- able workflow, reducing manual effort. The generated code handles background work like communication and payload parsing so that the developer can focus on implementing the application logic. The generation relies on Interface Description Lan- guages (IDLs) formally describing the API definition, including endpoints, addresses, data formats, and in- teraction patterns. Common examples encompass the OpenAPI Specification (OAS) [22], Protocol Buffers (Protobuf) [23], and Web Service Description Language (WSDL) [24]. Table I. Comparison of the features of the SDKs for working with the AAS v3 metamodel and AAS API. Explanation of the symbols: x= supported; E= experimental; P= partially supported; I= inherited from another SDK; C= client implementation; S= server implementation SDKModelClasses Serialization DeserializationAASXPackageHandlingVerification API ImplementationJSONXMLRDFJSONXMLRDFSubmodelServiceSubmodelRepositorySubmodelRegistryAASServiceAASRepositoryAASRegistryConceptDescr.RepositoryAASXFileServerBasicDiscovery Basyx .NET [9] x x x C,S C,S (C), S C,S C,S C,S S Basyx Python [10] x x x E x x x x S S Basyx Rust [11] x x x x Basyx Java V2 [12] I C C,S C,S C C,S C,S S S S AAS4J [13] x x x x x x x FAAAST [14] I S S S S S S S python-http-client [15] I C C C C C C C C C aas-core-works [16] C#, Go, (Micro)Python, Java, Rust, TS, C++ x x x x x P x In the following, we briefly present prominent tools fitting current and emerging AAS API implementations: a) OpenAPI Generator: is a widely used tool for generating boilerplate code for HTTP server implemen- tations [25]. Given an OpenAPI Description (OAD) as API definition, it produces the necessary payload classes, JSON de-/serialization logic for the payload, and stubs for the endpoints. This generator is the natural choice since the OAD is officially maintained for the AAS HTTP API definition [3]. Supported target languages include Java, C++, C#, Golang, Python, etc.. Addition- ally, it can transform the OAD into alternative IDLs like Protobuf or WSDL. However, this transformation carries risks, as differing feature sets among IDLs hinder a proper mapping of OAD-specific constructs. b) protoc: is the standard compiler for the gRPC protocol [26]. It uses Protobuf definitions to generate payload classes for the exchanged messages as well as client code and server stubs [27]. In contrast to HTTP and OpenAPI, the payload classes are primarily optimized for efficient binary serialization. The code can be generated for popular programming languages such as C#, C++, Python, and Go. c) SoapUI: is a tool that supports developers in implementing web applications [28]. It can also be used for automated API testing. Given a WSDL file, it generates server code in the desired target language, such as Java or C#. For code generation, SoapUI delegates the task to language-specific generators, e.g., Apache CXF [29] for Java. III. OVERVIEW OF THE CONCEPTS The conceptual approaches to implementing AAS applications using SDKs are summarized in Fig. 2. More concisely, in this work, we propose streamlined workflows that yield SDKs with comprehensive AAS metamodel and API support – ready to serve as the foundation for AAS applications. This section outlines the key characteristics of the workflows and explains their relation to existing implementation strategies. A. Fully Model-driven SDK Generation The first approach is entirely model-driven and fully automated. It builds upon the works of [4], popularized as the aas-core-codegen project [30]. This approach enhances the existing SDK generation pipeline in two major ways: 1) by formalizing the AAS API speci- fication, and 2) by implementing code generators for protocol- and language-specific server and client stubs in the target programming languages. The formalization process requires precisely inter- preting the official technology-agnostic and technology- specific AAS specifications [3] and encoding them alongside the formalized metamodel [31]. This includes all service specifications, endpoints, request mecha- nisms, and – ideally – pre- and post-conditions of all operations. A simplified example of such a formalization is given as pseudo-code in Listing 1. B. API Code Generators The second approach is a variation of the first but avoids implementing protocol- and language-specific Fig. 2. Overview of the conceptual workflows presented in this work. To highlight their differences, this chart combines all concepts and puts them into the context of existing workflows. The green dotted lines belong to approach B and are necessary implementations as explained in Section III. API stub generators by reusing existing tools (see Sec- tion II-C). It still relies on a formalized AAS API specification, but only requires code generators for the few desired IDLs [20]. These IDL generators produce protocol-specific API definitions, e.g., OAD or Protobuf, which can be processed by off-the-shelf generators to produce client and server stubs 1. For languages without structural typing, AAS SDK classes reside in separate units (modules, libraries, etc.) and cannot be directly used with code generated from IDLs. Even in structurally typed languages (e.g., Go, Python), the type definitions typically mismatch, as AAS SDK and IDL generators follow different conventions. Thus, the API stubs need to be complemented with established SDKs that handle non-API functions like verification. The resulting incompatible class structures require adapters or other integration mechanisms repre- sented by green dashed lines in Fig. 2. C. Customized API Code Generators The third approach aims to overcome the limitations of off-the-shelf API code generators, a topic explored in different contexts by [32]. Analogous to the previous approach, it uses the API definition generated from the formalized AAS API. However, a general-purpose open- source API generator is customized to better address AAS-specific constructs and SDKs. For example, the redundant generation of model classes or de/serialization logic can be avoided by reusing those from an AAS SDK if possible. Alternatively, payload classes can be gener- ated as slim wrappers around existing AAS SDK classes. 1The API definition can certainly be created manually, but this completely undermines the advantages of model-driven generation as API definition would need to be rewritten for each individual IDL. This preserves compatibility with protocol expectations while delegating complex validation or inheritance logic to the original SDK metamodel classes. Nonetheless, it will not be possible to fully replace the established SDK classes and functionalities with code generated by this custom generator. Due to the limited expressiveness of IDLs and inherent impedance mismatch [33], object-oriented structures – including recursive composite type definitions, complex inheri- tance hierarchies, and intricate constraints of the AAS metamodel – cannot always be described [34], [35]. D. Manual SDK Implementation In this approach, all SDK, client, and server function- alities are implemented manually. This involves reading and interpreting the AAS metamodel and API specifica- tion, then implementing model classes, de/serialization, verification, server stubs, and client code accordingly. As no generative step is involved, there is no need to implement adapters, bridge inconsistencies, or remove duplicates. The API implementation can be streamlined by lever- aging web frameworks such as FastAPI, Spring, or ASP.NET Core. These provide support for handling requests, registering endpoints, and organizing server stubs. They typically rely on declarative programming techniques, such as annotations or decorators, to allow developers to define and expose API endpoints. By abstracting away low-level infrastructure concerns, these frameworks let developers focus on the core application logic. Furthermore, some may automatically generate a formal API definition in a suitable IDL. This is the current practical approach for developing AAS applications (see Fig. 1); the previous three are # aas-core modules according to # aas-core-meta formalized model from aas_core3 import ( types as aastypes, verification as aasverification, jsonization as aasjsonization, ) class Response(Generic[T]): """Represent the response from an endpoint.""" ... @ensure( lambda shell, result: not aasverification.verify(shell) or result.code == 400, "Posting invalid shells results in a bad request." ) @snapshot( lambda self, shell: self.get_shell_by_id( id=shell.identifier) .code == 200, "Shell already exists." ) ... def post_shell( self, shell: aastypes.AssetAdministrationShell ) -> Response[ aastypes.AssetAdministrationShell ]: ... Listing 1: Pseudo-code demonstrating the declaration of pre- and post-conditions in a formalized AAS API specification. experimental. IV. QUALITATIVE EVALUATION This section evaluates the conceptual approaches pre- sented in Section III, especially regarding their risk for errors, effort when maintaining the workflow, as well as quality and usability of the resulting SDKs. We also assert how well the approaches integrate into existing SDKs (partly inspired by the summary of open-source AAS implementations [6]) and whether the implementa- tion of advanced AAS API features like pre- and post- conditions is possible. The findings are summarized in Table II. A. Fully Model-driven SDK Generation The primary advantage of this approach lies in the code generators tailored to the AAS domain and its Table II. Summary of the qualitative evaluation of the conceptual approaches of Section III. The symbols in the table cells indicate the performance/usability of a particular approach regarding the criteria in the table header. ++ means very good performance / easy to use; + means acceptable performance / usage possible; - means underwhelming performance / hard to use; -- means very poor performance / repeated use impossible ApproachSDKMaintainabilityToolchainMaintainabilityCommunitySupportInitialEffortVersioningEffortError-pronenessCross-languageConsistencyAdvancedAPIFeaturesExistingSDKIntegration A ++ + - -- ++ + ++ ++ ++ B + - + - + - ++ - - C + -- - - + - ++ + + D -- + - + -- -- -- ++ ++ formalized metamodel and API. This allows for precise coverage of the AAS semantics, ensuring that domain- specific requirements are captured properly. Such a gen- erator can produce all SDK functionality – including the existing model classes, de/serialization, and verification – without introducing redundant or inconsistent code artifacts. If the formal model encodes pre- and post- conditions of API operations, the generator can also transpile them and emit code to check them at runtime. This results in code that is easier to read, maintain, version, and update, making it a robust foundation for long-term SDK development. On the downside, implementing such a code generator is a complex manual programming effort. Although an initial implementation exists – namely, the aas-core- works project [16] – both the formalization [31] and the code generators [30] require extensions to support the AAS API specification. Moreover, since this toolchain is tailored to AAS, it is maintained by a smaller community (6 contributors in the aas-core-meta GitHub project and 8 in aas-core-codegen). This leads to less testing coverage and ecosystem support compared to other widely adopted technologies such as gRPC. B. API Code Generators Incorporating popular API code generators in the SDK and application development workflow leverages their large and active maintainer community. For example, the Protobuf toolchain [36] has over 1125 contributors, and the OpenAPI Generator [37] has more than 3226. Such broad support ensures reliable tooling with faster feature updates and quicker bug resolution. The main drawback of this approach is the need for adapters between AAS SDKs and code generated by general-purpose API toolchains. While some integration steps can be automated, others still require manual effort (see Section V). Also, generated code is often overly generic, making it harder to maintain. For SDK and application developers, this approach introduces further overhead: multiple generator toolchains must be man- aged, regularly updated, meticulously orchestrated, and their input files (the API definitions) kept in sync. C. Customized API Code Generators This approach allows developers to build on estab- lished API code generators with minimal intrusion while customizing the output to better align with existing AAS SDKs. For instance, a modified OpenAPI Generator could be configured to reuse SDK metamodel classes and AAS-specific JSON de/serialization functions instead of regenerating them. The customized generator can be ex- tended with additional features such as the generation of pre- and post-condition checks, as implemented in [38] and discussed in [39]. However, forking open-source API generators intro- duces the overhead of maintaining compatibility with the upstream changes. Integrating updates can become laborious and error-prone, given that the community for the customized generator is small. This approach, again, has to orchestrate multiple customized code generators and manage API definitions. D. Manual SDK Implementation Manual SDK implementation eliminates the need for formalized metamodels or API definitions. It yields intuitive and traceable code that mirrors the devel- oper’s understanding of the AAS specification. Modern web frameworks enhance structure and documentation, and existing codebases can be semantically patched if needed [40]. This approach, nonetheless, suffers from the well- known drawbacks of manual programming [41], includ- ing: 1) obscurity, 2) change amplification, 3) cognitive load, 4) errors due to misinterpretation, and 5) duplica- tion of logic. These issues stem from the tendency of low-level manual coding – such as directly translating the verbose AAS specification – to introduce acciden- tal complexity. In collaborative settings, inconsistent interpretations of the specification can lead to subtle and hard-to-trace bugs. Maintaining such code for one SDK is already demanding; doing so across multiple languages is nearly unmanageable, often resulting in divergent features and behavior. V. STUDY CASES We examine six implementations of AAS SDKs or applications and assess how they apply the concepts introduced in Section III. Fig. 3. Approach B with the necessary adapters and integration mechanisms as realized by many of the study cases presented in Section V. The first two cases are the Java SDKs for the AAS Basyx Java V2 and FAAAST, which provide API func- tionality and include server examples with basic business logic. Strictly speaking, both follow Approach D, imple- menting boilerplate server stubs manually and relying on the Spring framework for low-level request handling. However, for the JSON payload, they register so-called ObjectMappers, delegating to the de/serialization logic of the AAS4J SDK – which rather aligns with Approach B. The third case is the Basyx .NET SDK for C#, again offering extensive API support and server demonstrators. The server stubs closely resemble code generated by the OpenAPI Generator, suggesting adherence to Ap- proach B – based on the official hand-written OAD for the AAS API. However, the generated payload models appear to have been replaced by custom metamodel classes. Using ASP.NET and annotations of that frame- work, these classes are de/serialized via standard C# mechanisms, bypassing the SDK’s own AAS-specific de/serialization logic. The AASX-Server [42] serves as the fourth example, as it aims to be the reference implementation for the AAS API. It employs server stubs produced by the OpenAPI Generator and integrates the aas-core-csharp SDK for metamodel support. Following Approach B, the generated payload classes are replaced with SDK classes, and de/serialization logic is delegated to the SDK logic using so-called InputFormatters. The fifth example is the AAS Manager [43], which is powered by the Basyx Python SDK. This SDK provides ready-to-use server implementations that are directly in- tegrated into the AAS Manager. The metamodel classes and de/serialization logic in the SDK are entirely hand- written, making this a clear instance of Approach D. The final example, originally proposed as Multi- Message-Broker in [44] and further elaborated in [7], introduces an application with HTTP and gRPC inter- faces exposing the AAS API. AAS metamodel types are mapped to Protobuf messages as described in [27]. The gRPC endpoints are derived from the AAS OAD using the OpenAPI Generator, with semi-automatic removal of redundant message definitions. Special conversion func- tions are generated by aas-core-codegen so that Protobuf classes with embedded specialized de/serialization can be used together with SDK metamodel classes [27]. For the HTTP interface, the JSON de/serialization and payload classes are replaced by the logic of the aas-core- csharp SDK. It is therefore a realization of Approach B with more involved adaptation mechanisms as can be seen in Fig. 3. VI. DISCUSSION The concepts presented in this work are inspired by the popular open-source AAS SDKs and other code generation tools commonly used by developers. They are thus a snapshot of the rapidly evolving tool landscape. We highlight trade-offs between manual, code- generated, and entirely model-driven approaches to AAS SDK and application development. Tailored model- driven generators provide significantly better maintain- ability and correctness at scale than manual implemen- tations, which is in line with the general discussion of software design [41]. Such generative workflows do, however, demand vast up-front effort and skilled devel- opers. Therefore, model-driven generation is not justified for a small development team with limited resources working on a single-platform, single-language project. In these scenarios, general-purpose API code generators pose a viable alternative. The examination of our study cases allows us to eval- uate the practical application of the proposed concepts. However, this assessment relies on the interpretation of codebases and documentation, which may introduce bias. The examples reveal that combining multiple code generators in a project requires elaborate integration strategies and thorough code clean-ups. Nevertheless, we find that such semi-automatic workflows improve code correctness and reduce programming effort, making them viable for AAS application development. Fully manual implementations are declining. Yet, comprehensive end- to-end model-driven, AAS-specific code generation re- mains absent. Given the significant up-front effort, this is unsurprising. Still, generative workflows offer clear advantages: straightforward propagation of minor and medium updates to the AAS metamodel and API into downstream SDKs of applications. Only major structural changes require adaptations in the code generators, as highlighted by [45]. This work, however, lacks results to directly quantify the time spendings and error rates of the respective approaches. Only implicit conclusions can be drawn by comparing the compliance of hand-written and model- driven AAS SDKs, as shown in [5]. A detailed quantita- tive evaluation is pending and planned for future work. VII. CONCLUSION AND FUTURE WORK In this work, we have analyzed existing tools and SDKs supporting the development of AAS applications. We worked out concepts for combining these tools in workflows of varying degrees of automation and complexity. We have evaluated the presented concepts qualitatively regarding their implementation effort, com- munity support, maintainability, and long-term stability at scale. The applicability of the concepts has been ascertained on established AAS SDKs and applications that served as study cases. This work demonstrated how multiple code genera- tors can be successfully combined in a semi-automatic implementation workflow. We were therefore able to conceptually show how the use of different tools in model-driven workflows overcomes the disadvantages of entirely manual implementations. To optimize the AAS application development by reducing tool fragmentation, we recommend investing in shared, entirely model-driven code generation pipelines. Future work should extend the promising results of model-driven AAS SDK development to support the AAS API specification. We will then be able to quantita- tively compare the implementation workflows regarding the time saved by code generators, the effort spent in creating the generator pipeline, and the correctness of the resulting generated code.
